messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] R-sig-Epi Digest, Vol 17, Issue 2
messages.body_plain:
On 9/26/07 Tom Willems <Tom.Willems at var.fgov.be> wrote:

> Subject: [R-sig-Epi] Q: Transmission rate by martingale methode
>         (dejong.R)
> To: r-sig-epi at stat.math.ethz.ch
> Message-ID:
>         <OF99B7A90A.9FD066D8-ONC1257361.004507EC-C1257361.00469F90 at var.fgov.be>
>
> Content-Type: text/plain
>
> good afternoon R-ussers,
>
> I am looking for means to calculate the transmission rate of a virus.
> Reading trough many articles, i found the martingale methode to be the
> most commen ussed.

When giving citations it is useful to give complete details.


> An article even points me to the exisrence of an R file, that handles
> these estimations.
> It would be the file named :  <  dejong.R  >.
> Now since i could not find this file i hoped it would be implemented in
> the Epi library,
> Yet I did not find anything on calculating Transmission Rates (R0) or
> infection rates.
> Can someone pleas point me to where the dejong.R file may be found, or a
> file handeling this estimations?

A search for the terms 'dejong.R martingale'
(http://www.google.co.uk/search?num=100&hl=en&safe=off&q=dejong.R+martingale&btnG=Search&meta=)
points to five documents, the first of which is the most appropriate
(the remainder are about an author Dejong R).

Reading thought the first pdf it indicates that the dejong.R file that
you are interested in does the calculations.  This pdf is hosted as
part of the homepage for Ass Prof Michael H?hle at University of
Munich.  If you traverse to a slightly higher level of his homepage
(http://www.stat.uni-muenchen.de/~hoehle/) you'll see a link to
"Statistical Software". Follow this and you get to what you are after,
which is the fourth item under the Epidemic Modelling.  This is a zip
file that contains the dejong.R file that you are seeking.


Regards

Neil
-- 
"In mathematics you don't understand things. You just get used to
them."  - Johann von Neumann

Email - nshephard at gmail.com / n.shephard at sheffield.ac.uk
Website - http://slack.ser.man.ac.uk/
Photos - http://www.flickr.com/photos/slackline/

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] About parametric survival analysis
messages.body_plain:
Good afternoon R-users!
I have a question about survival analysis.
I have a set of data for which I calculate non parametric survival
function using the classical Kaplan-Meier estimation.

Data are in the following form

date of birth, date of exit, censor


Now I want to compare the "Kaplan-Meier" curve with a parametric survival
curve (a Weibull seems to be a reasonable choice in my example). Is there
a function in R to do this?
Thanks a lot!

Martina Gandini
messages.body_snippet:

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Q: Transmission rate by martingale methode (dejong.R)
messages.subject_clean: Q: Transmission rate by martingale methode (dejong.R)
messages.body_plain:
Hi Tom!
Googling

dejong.R martingale transmission rate

pointed me to the pdf-file:
http://www.stat.uni-muenchen.de/~hoehle/pubs/r0moment.pdf. Browsing
Michael H?hle's web page: http://www.stat.uni-muenchen.de/~hoehle I
found the 'dejong.R' file on his "Statistical Software page":

http://www.stat.uni-muenchen.de/~hoehle/software/epi/DeJong.zip

HTH
/Johan




Tom Willems skrev:
> good afternoon R-ussers,
> 
> I am looking for means to calculate the transmission rate of a virus.
> Reading trough many articles, i found the martingale methode to be the 
> most commen ussed.
> An article even points me to the exisrence of an R file, that handles 
> these estimations.
> It would be the file named :  <  dejong.R  >.
> Now since i could not find this file i hoped it would be implemented in 
> the Epi library,
> Yet I did not find anything on calculating Transmission Rates (R0) or 
> infection rates.
> Can someone pleas point me to where the dejong.R file may be found, or a 
> file handeling this estimations?
> 
> thank you very much,
> Tom.
> 
> 
> Disclaimer: click here
> 	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
> 
> _______________________________________________
> R-sig-Epi at stat.math.ethz.ch mailing list
> https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-sig-epi

-- 
  O    |>  Johan Lindb?ck,  Biostatistician
 /\    |   Uppsala Clinical Research Center
 | \_.     SE-751 85  UPPSALA
~~~~~~~~~~ <http://www.ucr.uu.se> ----------

> Subject: [R-sig-Epi] How to calculate the nth derivative of a function
> 
> Hi,
> 
> I have a short question regarding derivatives of functions in 
> R. The problem is that I need the nth derivative of the 
> function, where n is around 100, and the packages I have 
> managed to find which deals with derivatives in R will only 
> take the first derivative of the function. Repeating these 
> methods a 100 times won't solve the problem either, since the 
> first derivative of a numerical function gives me a scalar as 
> output. Does anyone know how to solve this problem? I'm 
> grateful for any advice!
> 
> Patricia
> 
> 	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
> 
> _______________________________________________
> R-sig-Epi at stat.math.ethz.ch mailing list 
> https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-sig-epi
>

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] How to calculate the nth derivative of a function
messages.subject_clean: How to calculate the nth derivative of a function
messages.body_plain:
You may want to look at the Ryacas package.  This uses and outside
toolset to do the work, but in this case I think that is the better
approach.

-- 
Gregory (Greg) L. Snow Ph.D.
Statistical Data Center
Intermountain Healthcare
greg.snow at intermountainmail.org
(801) 408-8111

> From: r-sig-epi-bounces at stat.math.ethz.ch

>

> R. The problem is that I need the nth derivative of the 
> function, where n is around 100, and the packages I have 
> managed to find which deals with derivatives in R will only 
> take the first derivative of the function. Repeating these 
> methods a 100 times won't solve the problem either, since the 
> first derivative of a numerical function gives me a scalar as 
> output. Does anyone know how to solve this problem? I'm 
> grateful for any advice!
> 
> Patricia

You may want to look at the Ryacas package.  This uses and outside
toolset to do the work, but in this case I think that is the better
approach.

--
